network.overall_strategy: This network targets glycine through ten interconnected prebiotic routes spanning hydrothermal carbon fixation, surface cyanide and carbonyl chemistry, and biochemical/prebiotic conversion steps. The network is built around hub intermediates shared across environments: carbon dioxide, formate, formaldehyde, hydrogen cyanide, glyoxylate, and glycine itself. Hydrothermal reactions generate reduced C1-C3 feedstocks such as formate, acetate, pyruvate, formaldehyde, and glycolaldehyde on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide minerals. Surface environments use these feedstocks in HCN photochemistry, Strecker-type synthesis, formose/aldol chemistry, and oxidation to glyoxylate. Biochemical/prebiotic steps connect α-keto acid and aminonitrile chemistry to the final amino acid pool and peptide-forming context. The resulting bipartite graph provides multiple convergent and redundant routes to glycine while preserving full network connectivity and cross-environment transfer.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduction of carbon dioxide to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated CO2 adsorption and stepwise two-electron reduction by H2 to surface-bound formyl/formate equivalents released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Fe-S minerals are widely invoked as catalysts for CO2 reduction under alkaline hydrothermal conditions, making formate a key reduced C1 feedstock and network hub in iron-sulfur world chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Martin, 2004; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal conversion of formate to formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Further reduction of C1 oxoanion species on reduced iron surfaces yields methanal after dehydration/protonation steps.
reactions.reasoning: Reduced iron and magnetite promote hydrogenation of oxidized carbon species under hydrothermal conditions; use of formaldehyde as a transferable C1 intermediate is consistent with hydrothermal-to-surface coupling although exact yields remain environment dependent (He et al., 2010; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type formation of glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal vent or subseafloor setting

reactions.mechanism: CO2-derived reduced C1 units undergo surface carbon-carbon coupling and partial reduction to give a C2 aldehyde, glycolaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type and related hydrothermal catalytic processes on iron minerals can generate low-molecular-weight oxygenates including aldehydes; glycolaldehyde is a plausible transferable sugar precursor feeding surface chemistry (McCollom & Seewald, 2007; Rushdi & Simoneit, 2001).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate from CO2 and H2

conditions.other: alkaline vent redox and pH gradients

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation and carbon-carbon bond formation produce an α-keto acid analogous to reductive acetyl-CoA/reductive TCA chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a central α-keto acid in prebiotic protometabolism and has been discussed as a plausible hydrothermal product of Fe-S mediated carbon fixation networks, though specific routes remain partly model-driven (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate forms an imine/iminium with ammonia on mineral surfaces followed by reduction to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids under hydrothermal mineral catalysis is a standard prebiotic route to amino acids and links carbon fixation chemistry to amino acid inventories (Huber & Wachtershauser, 2006; Muchowska et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of HCN from reduced C1 feedstocks
reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photocatalytic oxidation-reduction and dehydration of reduced C1/N species yields nitrile functionality, furnishing HCN as a reactive nitrogen-carbon hub.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is central to surface prebiotic synthesis networks; TiO2-assisted UV photochemistry and sulfur-assisted cyanide generation are used here as a plausible coupling of reduced hydrothermal feedstocks to cyanosulfidic surface chemistry (Sutherland, 2016; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface HCN hydration to formamide
reactions.mechanism: HCN is hydrated in the presence of mineral surfaces to formamide, a versatile precursor pool for further N-C chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide chemistry is strongly represented in prebiotic synthesis scenarios and can arise from cyanide hydrolysis/hydration under surface conditions; mineral surfaces enhance concentration and subsequent transformations (Saladino et al., 2012; Ferus et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface partial hydrolysis/reduction of HCN to methanimine
reactions.mechanism: Activated nitrile chemistry followed by proton-coupled reduction furnishes the imine methanimine.
reactions.reasoning: Methanimine is a recognized intermediate in prebiotic amino acid chemistry and can bridge cyanide chemistry to amino nitriles; this step is plausible though less directly constrained than Strecker addition itself (Danger et al., 2012; Woon, 2002).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine that is attacked by cyanide to give aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is among the most established prebiotic routes to amino acid precursors. For glycine specifically, formaldehyde plus NH3 and HCN gives aminoacetonitrile as the direct nitrile precursor (Miller, 1957; Peltzer et al., 1984).

reactions.name: Surface aminoacetonitrile formation from methanimine and HCN
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic cyanide addition to methanimine yields the aminonitrile aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Amino nitriles can form by direct cyanide addition to imines; this provides a second convergent route to aminoacetonitrile from the HCN hub and is chemically aligned with Strecker-type logic (Danger et al., 2012; Bernstein et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Surface oxidation of glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-irradiated pool; borate stabilizes carbonyl species

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox oxidation of the aldehyde/alcohol functionality of glycolaldehyde gives the α-keto acid glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is a plausible oxidation product of simple aldehydes in surface settings and is a useful hub linking sugar chemistry to amino acid chemistry; borate can stabilize reactive carbonyl intermediates in these networks (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Ricardo et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Surface oxidative fragmentation of pyruvate to glyoxylate
reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation/decarboxylative rearrangement of pyruvate furnishes glyoxylate under surface redox cycling.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate and glyoxylate are interconvertible within abiotic carbonyl networks, and pyruvate photochemistry provides a second route into the glyoxylate hub, linking hydrothermal α-keto acid production to surface amino acid synthesis (Muchowska et al., 2019; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface borate-stabilized formose step to glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldol-type carbon-carbon bond formation between reduced carbonyl compounds yields glyceraldehyde, stabilized against runaway formose degradation by borate.
reactions.reasoning: Borate-assisted sugar chemistry is a central strategy for stabilizing triose products in prebiotic surface environments, and glyceraldehyde acts as a branching point into oxidation and retro-aldol chemistry (Ricardo et al., 2004; Benner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Surface retro-aldol/oxidative conversion of glyceraldehyde to glyoxylate
reactions.mechanism: Photooxidative fragmentation of the triose yields a C2 α-keto acid pool including glyoxylate while regenerating formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Dynamic carbonyl exchange in surface sugar chemistry allows feedback between triose and C1/C2 pools, strengthening network connectivity; this step is plausible but partly network-completion oriented (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; speculative integration).

reactions.name: Surface HCN dimerization to cyanogen

conditions.other: UV photochemistry in concentrated cyanide pools

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical radical coupling/oxidative dimerization of cyanide gives cyanogen.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization and related cyanide coupling reactions are classical features of prebiotic cyanide chemistry, and cyanogen-like species can feed further aminonitrile derivatization (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Sanchez et al., 1967).

reactions.name: Surface conversion of cyanogen and ammonia to aminoacetonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Activated dinitrile chemistry followed by ammonolysis and reduction gives aminoacetonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is a more speculative but chemically motivated alternative route to the glycine aminonitrile hub from cyanide oligomer chemistry, included to create pathway redundancy around aminoacetonitrile (speculative integration based on cyanide oligomer literature: Ferris & Orgel, 1966).

reactions.name: Surface formamide-cyanide route to aminoacetonitrile derivative
reactions.mechanism: Formamide-derived amidation/cyanation chemistry yields an activated glycine-related nitrile-amide intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide networks can access nitrile-containing intermediates relevant to amino acid synthesis; this step is included as a plausible bridge from HCN hydration chemistry to glycine precursors though exact structure/yield is less established (Saladino et al., 2012; speculative integration).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolytic conversion of activated glycine nitrile-amide to glycine
reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile and amide hydrolysis furnishes the amino acid carboxylate/ammonium pair identified as glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Mineral-assisted hydrolysis of aminonitrile-type precursors to amino acids is broadly accepted in prebiotic chemistry and provides a direct route from activated nitrile-amide pools to glycine (Peltzer et al., 1984; Danger et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles promote nitrile hydration and completion to amino acid

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide and acid stages to yield glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile is the canonical completion step of Strecker chemistry to glycine and is strongly supported in prebiotic amino acid synthesis literature (Miller, 1957; Peltzer et al., 1984).

reactions.name: Surface reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: reducing pool with NH3 and dissolved H2 equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Glyoxylate forms an imine with ammonia followed by reduction to the α-amino acid glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids is a direct and metabolically relevant path to amino acids. Glyoxylate is the C2 analogue that gives glycine, providing a route independent of Strecker chemistry (Muchowska et al., 2017; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic transamination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: concentrated prebiotic pool or mineral interface

reactions.mechanism: Amino transfer from alanine to glyoxylate generates glycine and regenerates pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Transamination is a key network-connecting process that links hydrothermally generated alanine and pyruvate to glycine synthesis through glyoxylate, creating a plausible proto-metabolic loop even if uncatalyzed rates are modest (Weber & Miller, 1981; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical decarboxylation/oxidative shortening of pyruvate to glycine

conditions.other: geothermal cycling; speculative multi-step net conversion

reactions.mechanism: Net conversion invokes pyruvate decarboxylation to a C2 carbonyl followed by amination and oxidation-state adjustment to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: This is a speculative net step included to represent a proto-metabolic shortening route from pyruvate to glycine in geothermal pools. It is less directly evidenced than Strecker or glyoxylate amination routes but helps capture plausible network flexibility around α-keto acid chemistry (speculative integration informed by protometabolic carbonyl chemistry: Muchowska et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker route from hydrothermal formaldehyde
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction generates formate and formaldehyde, which feed a surface Strecker synthesis to aminoacetonitrile followed by hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Direct formaldehyde Strecker without explicit HCN regeneration loop
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde enters the surface environment and directly undergoes Strecker synthesis with pre-existing HCN and ammonia, then hydrolysis to glycine.

pathways.name: Methanimine-mediated aminonitrile route
pathways.description: Surface cyanide is reduced to methanimine, which captures cyanide to form aminoacetonitrile and then hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanogen branch to aminoacetonitrile
pathways.description: HCN dimerizes to cyanogen, which in a reducing ammoniacal pool yields aminoacetonitrile, then glycine by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Formamide-derived nitrile-amide route
pathways.description: Surface HCN hydrates to formamide, then enters a cyanation route to an activated glycine-related nitrile-amide intermediate that hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal glycolaldehyde to surface glyoxylate to glycine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glycolaldehyde is transferred to the surface, photooxidized to glyoxylate, then reductively aminated to glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to surface glyoxylate to glycine
pathways.description: Pyruvate generated hydrothermally is photochemically converted to glyoxylate in surface pools and then aminated to glycine.

pathways.name: Borate-stabilized sugar route to glyoxylate and glycine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde feed surface borate-stabilized sugar chemistry to glyceraldehyde, which fragments oxidatively to glyoxylate and then gives glycine by reductive amination.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alanine to biochemical transamination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is aminated to alanine; surface or hydrothermal pyruvate also supplies glyoxylate, and biochemical transamination between alanine and glyoxylate yields glycine.

pathways.name: Proto-metabolic shortening of pyruvate to glycine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate enters a biochemical/prebiotic geothermal pool where net decarboxylative and aminating transformations furnish glycine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Conceptually strong canonical Strecker route with good hydrothermal-to-surface flow. The key weakness is the proposed generation of HCN directly from formaldehyde and ammonia, which is not a well-supported prebiotic transformation under stated surface conditions. If an exogenous or separately generated HCN source is assumed, the downstream Strecker and hydrolysis steps are among the most plausible routes to glycine in the network.
suggestions: Replace rxn_006 with a more plausible HCN source such as atmospheric/photochemical cyanide delivery or ferrocyanide/cyanosulfidic chemistry
Clarify whether rxn_002 is intended as stepwise CO2 reduction via methanol-equivalent intermediates rather than direct formate-to-formaldehyde conversion
Specify mineral agents for all hydrothermal and surface reactions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest route if HCN is treated as an available hub molecule rather than requiring in-network synthesis from formaldehyde. Strecker synthesis from formaldehyde to aminoacetonitrile followed by hydrolysis to glycine is chemically credible and convergent. Remaining concerns are the underspecified origin of HCN and the questionable direct reduction of formate to formaldehyde.
suggestions: Make the HCN source explicit and independent of formaldehyde-ammonia chemistry
Add hydrolysis conditions for aminoacetonitrile to strengthen plausibility
Include mineral catalysts explicitly for surface Strecker and hydrolysis context

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The methanimine-mediated branch is chemically imaginable, but the route to methanimine from HCN and H2 under surface conditions is weakly supported and likely oversimplified. Once methanimine is available, addition of HCN to give aminoacetonitrile is plausible. Overall, the pathway is coherent but relies heavily on speculative upstream chemistry.
suggestions: Replace rxn_008 with a more defensible imine-forming route from formaldehyde and ammonia
Use methanimine as a downstream equilibrium species in Strecker chemistry rather than a separately synthesized node
Provide experimental precedent for surface reduction chemistry with stated minerals

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the weakest branches. The assignment of HCN dimerization to cyanogen is chemically incorrect or at least highly misleading, and conversion of cyanogen plus ammonia and hydrogen directly to aminoacetonitrile is poorly grounded. Although hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine is plausible, the branch leading to the aminonitrile is not well supported.
suggestions: Remove the cyanogen branch unless supported by specific literature
If retained, redefine cyanogen formation and downstream chemistry with realistic intermediates
Use more established cyanide oligomerization products rather than cyanogen as a glycine precursor

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN hydration to formamide is plausible, but the formamide-cyanide route to an activated glycinenitrile-amide analogue is insufficiently specified and appears speculative. The final hydrolytic collapse to glycine may be possible in broad terms, but the intermediate itself is not well defined. This pathway is weak without stronger mechanistic or literature support.
suggestions: Define mol_017 more rigorously and justify its prebiotic relevance
Support the formamide-cyanide branch with literature precedent or remove it
Favor direct aminonitrile hydrolysis over poorly specified nitrile-amide analogues

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a good alternative non-cyanide route. Hydrothermal formation of glycolaldehyde is plausible in broad Fischer-Tropsch-type terms but often low-yield and condition-sensitive. Oxidation to glyoxylate and reductive amination to glycine are reasonable prebiotic proposals, giving this pathway strong coherence and strong environmental interconnection.
suggestions: Specify the hydrothermal catalyst and pressure-temperature window for glycolaldehyde production
Clarify the oxidant or photochemical/mineral mechanism for glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate
State whether reductive amination proceeds on metal sulfides, Fe0, or photoredox mineral surfaces

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pathway is topologically neat but chemically strained. Direct pyruvate formation from CO2 and H2 in hydrothermal settings remains speculative, and oxidative fragmentation of pyruvate to glyoxylate is not a standard or well-supported prebiotic route. The final reductive amination of glyoxylate is plausible, but the preceding steps weaken the overall route.
suggestions: Replace direct pyruvate synthesis with a more stepwise alpha-keto acid network
Substitute pyruvate-to-glyoxylate fragmentation with better supported glyoxylate sources
Provide explicit mineral and redox context for alpha-keto acid generation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The borate-stabilized sugar chemistry is plausible and improves network diversity, but the proposed conversion of glyceraldehyde to glyoxylate plus formaldehyde is an oversimplified and insufficiently supported oxidative retro-aldol step. This route is interesting and connected, yet it depends on an uncertain cleavage/oxidation transformation.
suggestions: Support glyceraldehyde-to-glyoxylate conversion with a specific photochemical or mineral-mediated mechanism
Consider alternative routes from sugar chemistry to glycine precursors, such as glyoxal or aminodiol chemistry
Retain borate-assisted aldol chemistry but strengthen downstream oxidation logic

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The transamination logic is strong: alanine and glyoxylate are a credible prebiotic amino transfer pair, and the biochemical step is one of the most plausible in the network. However, this branch inherits the weaknesses of direct pyruvate synthesis and pyruvate-to-glyoxylate conversion. With better upstream support, this could become one of the best pathways.
suggestions: Keep the transamination endpoint but source glyoxylate from glycolaldehyde oxidation rather than pyruvate fragmentation
Use a more defensible pyruvate formation route or supply pyruvate from a separate network branch
Specify mineral or cofactor analogues enabling transamination in the biochemical stage

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is not chemically convincing. The direct conversion of pyruvate and ammonia to glycine via decarboxylation/oxidative shortening is not a standard prebiotic transformation and is inadequately justified mechanistically. It functions more as a placeholder than a robust route to the target.
suggestions: Remove or relabel rxn_022 as speculative
Replace with a validated route through glyoxylate, formaldehyde, or aminoacetonitrile
Avoid unsupported one-step carbon-skeleton shortening to glycine

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong: it is densely connected, contains more than enough molecule and reaction nodes, and uses several effective hubs including HCN, formaldehyde, pyruvate, glyoxylate, and aminoacetonitrile. Convergence is a clear strength, with multiple branches meeting at glyoxylate and aminoacetonitrile before reaching glycine. Environmental interconnection is also good in concept, especially hydrothermal generation of reduced feedstocks feeding surface chemistry and then biochemical transamination. However, chemical quality is uneven. Several core routes are plausible, especially Strecker hydrolysis and glyoxylate reductive amination/transamination, but multiple reactions are weak, speculative, or chemically problematic: formaldehyde-to-HCN generation, HCN-to-methanimine reduction, HCN-to-cyanogen dimerization, cyanogen-to-aminoacetonitrile conversion, pyruvate-to-glyoxylate fragmentation, and pyruvate-to-glycine shortening. A further major formal issue is that the network summary does not specify the required mineral agents for hydrothermal and surface reactions, which is a significant compliance gap relative to the design constraints. Overall, this is a creative and well-connected synthesis network with partial target achievement, but it falls short of robust approval at the full-network level because too many branches depend on undersupported chemistry. The strongest subnetwork is the hydrothermal glycolaldehyde to surface glyoxylate to glycine route, followed by Strecker chemistry when HCN availability is assumed externally.
